1:
"And he made the altar of burnt offering of shittim wood: five cubits was the length thereof, and five cubits the breadth thereof; it was foursquare; and three cubits the height thereof." The witnesses note the making of the altar of burnt offering — the crux of the place of sacrifice and the figure of the cross. On "he made the altar of burnt offering of shittim wood... it was foursquare," the grammatical voices note the altar. Henry and Gill note that "he made the altar of burnt offering of shittim wood: five cubits was the length thereof, and five cubits the breadth thereof; it was foursquare; and three cubits the height thereof" — the brazen altar of burnt offering was made of acacia wood overlaid with brass (vv.2), five cubits square and three high, set in the court; the altar (the great altar of sacrifice at the entrance of the court, Exodus 27:1-8); the foursquare (the altar square, the figure of the four corners of the earth, the sacrifice for all); the place of sacrifice (where the burnt offerings were offered, the blood shed, the atonement made).

On the altar, interpreters expound the figure. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the place of atonement — the altar of burnt offering, where the sacrifices were offered for the expiation of sin; the witnesses note the figure — the altar a figure of the cross of Christ, the place of the great sacrifice (Christ "offered himself," Hebrews 9:14; the altar that sanctifies the gift, Matthew 23:19; the cross the true altar where the Lamb of God was offered); the brass (the altar overlaid with brass, enduring the fire, the figure of Christ enduring the fire of God's wrath, bearing the judgment for sin); the burnt offering (the whole offering, wholly consumed, the figure of Christ's entire self-offering, Ephesians 5:2); the first altar met at the entrance (no approach to God but by the altar of sacrifice, by the blood, Hebrews 9:22; the cross the way of access).

On the making, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the sacrifice. Wesley and Clarke note the altar of burnt offering (the place of the sacrifices, the expiation) — the figure (the cross, the place of atonement); the providence (the altar made, the figure of the great sacrifice; no approach to God but by the altar). The verse displays the making of the altar of burnt offering.

The witnesses agree the substance: the altar of burnt offering was made of acacia overlaid with brass, foursquare, the place of sacrifice (Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the place of sacrifice: the altar of burnt offering — the great altar at the entrance of the court (Exodus 27:1-8), the place of the sacrifices and the expiation of sin, a figure of the cross of Christ where the Lamb of God was offered (Hebrews 9:14; Ephesians 5:2), the brass enduring the fire of God's wrath, no approach to God but by the altar of sacrifice (Hebrews 9:22). The verse displays the altar crux. The verse marks the altar: of burnt offering. The verse displays the sacrifice: the expiation. The verse displays the brass: enduring fire. The verse displays the cross: figured. The making displays the place of sacrifice and atonement. The verse displays the altar: made. The verse displays the sacrifice: offered. The verse displays the cross: the true altar. The making of the altar of burnt offering displays the crux: the altar of burnt offering, foursquare (Exodus 27:1-8; Hebrews 9:14; Ephesians 5:2) — the place of sacrifice and atonement, a figure of the cross where Christ offered himself, the way of access to God. The verse displays the overlaying and vessels (vv.2-7).
3:
"And he made all the vessels of the altar, the pots, and the shovels, and the basons, and the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all the vessels thereof made he of brass." The very brief verse notes the making of the vessels of the altar. The grammatical voice notes the vessels. Ellicott notes that "he made all the vessels of the altar, the pots, and the shovels, and the basons, and the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all the vessels thereof made he of brass" — all the vessels of the brazen altar (pots for ashes, shovels, basins for blood, fleshhooks, firepans) were made of brass; the vessels (the instruments for the service of the altar, Exodus 27:3); the brass (all of brass, the metal of the altar and its furniture); the service (the vessels for handling the ashes, the blood, the flesh, and the fire).

On "the pots, and the shovels, and the basons," the witness notes the instruments. Ellicott notes the pots, shovels, and basins (for the ashes and the blood, Exodus 27:3) — the service (the instruments of the sacrifice).

On "the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all the vessels thereof made he of brass," the grammatical-historical note the brass: "the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all the vessels thereof made he of brass" — the fleshhooks (for the flesh) and firepans (for the coals), all of brass; the providence (the vessels of the altar, all of brass, the furniture of the sacrifice; the figure of the various means and instruments of the one sacrifice). The verse displays the making of the vessels of the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the vessels — "all the vessels of the altar, the pots, and the shovels, and the basons, and the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all... of brass," the instruments (Exodus 27:3, Ellicott); "the pots, and the shovels, and the basons," the instruments (Ellicott); "the fleshhooks, and the firepans: all the vessels thereof made he of brass," the brass (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the altar vessels. The verse marks the vessels: of brass. The verse displays the pots: and shovels. The verse displays the basons: and firepans. The verse displays the service: of the altar. The vessels display the furniture of the sacrifice. The verse displays the vessels: made. The verse displays the brass: throughout. The verse displays the service: equipped. The making of the vessels of the altar displays the furniture of the sacrifice: the pots, shovels, basons, fleshhooks, and firepans of brass (Exodus 27:3) — all the vessels of the brazen altar, the instruments for the service of the sacrifice. The verse displays the grate and rings (vv.4-7).
5:
"And he cast four rings for the four ends of the grate of brass, to be places for the staves." The very brief verse notes the rings for the staves of the altar. The grammatical voice notes the rings. JFB notes that "he cast four rings for the four ends of the grate of brass, to be places for the staves" — four brass rings were cast at the four ends of the grate, as holders for the staves (poles) to carry the altar; the rings (the holders for the staves, Exodus 27:4); the staves (the poles for bearing the altar, the altar portable); the bearing (the altar carried on the journey, like the ark, the place of sacrifice moving with the people).

On "he cast four rings for the four ends of the grate of brass," the witness notes the rings. JFB notes the four rings (for the staves, the altar made portable, Exodus 27:4-5) — the bearing (the altar carried, the sacrifice ever with the people on the way).

On "to be places for the staves," the grammatical-historical note the staves: "to be places for the staves" — the rings for the staves (the altar borne by the poles); the figure (the altar of sacrifice moving with the pilgrim people, the cross ever before them); the providence (the altar made portable, the place of sacrifice with the people in the wilderness). The verse displays the rings for the staves of the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the rings — "he cast four rings for the four ends of the grate of brass, to be places for the staves," the bearing (Exodus 27:4, JFB); "he cast four rings for the four ends of the grate of brass," the rings (Exodus 27:4-5, JFB); "to be places for the staves," the staves (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the altar rings. The verse marks the rings: four. The verse displays the staves: the poles. The verse displays the altar: portable. The verse displays the sacrifice: borne. The rings display the altar made to be carried. The verse displays the rings: cast. The verse displays the altar: borne. The verse displays the sacrifice: with the people. The rings for the staves of the altar display the portable altar: four rings for the staves (Exodus 27:4-5) — the altar made portable, the place of sacrifice borne with the pilgrim people. The verse displays the staves and the hollow altar (vv.6-7).
8:
"And he made the laver of brass, and the foot of it of brass, of the lookingglasses of the women assembling, which assembled at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation." The witnesses note the laver made of the women's mirrors — the crux of cleansing and self-denial. On "he made the laver of brass, and the foot of it of brass, of the lookingglasses of the women assembling," interpreters expound the laver. The dominant reading and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the making — "he made the laver of brass... of the lookingglasses of the women assembling, which assembled at the door of the tabernacle": the brazen laver (the basin for the priests' washing, Exodus 30:18-21) was made of the brass mirrors (looking-glasses, polished brass mirrors, not glass) freely given by the women who assembled (served, ministered, or gathered) at the door of the tabernacle; the witnesses note the significance — the women's mirrors (the instruments of vanity and self-adornment) given up and made into the laver of cleansing; the figure — the self-denial of the women (renouncing their vanity, surrendering their mirrors for God's house, the figure of laying aside self-love and vanity for God); and the laver itself a figure of cleansing — the washing of the priests (the figure of the washing of regeneration and sanctification, Titus 3:5; Ephesians 5:26, "the washing of water by the word"); and notably the word of God as a mirror (the laver of brass, once mirrors, the figure of the word that both reflects us to ourselves and cleanses us, James 1:23-25, "a man beholding his natural face in a glass"; the word the mirror that shows our defilement and the laver that washes it; 2 Corinthians 3:18, beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord).

On "the women assembling, which assembled at the door of the tabernacle," the witnesses note the women. Clarke and Ellicott note the women who assembled (served, ministered) at the door of the tabernacle (the godly women, devoted to God's service, who gave their mirrors) — the devotion (the women's service and self-denial, giving their vanity to God); the figure (the surrender of self for God's house).

On the laver, the grammatical-historical note the cleansing: the laver of brass — the place of washing; the providence (the laver of cleansing, made of the surrendered mirrors, the figure of self-denial and the cleansing word). The verse displays the laver made of the women's mirrors.

The witnesses agree the substance: the laver was made of the brass mirrors of the women who assembled at the door (Wesley, Clarke, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of cleansing and self-denial: the laver of the women's mirrors — the instruments of vanity surrendered and made into the laver of cleansing (the self-denial of the women, renouncing their vanity for God's house), the laver a figure of the washing of regeneration and sanctification (Titus 3:5; Ephesians 5:26), the word a mirror that shows and cleanses our defilement (James 1:23-25). The verse displays the laver crux. The verse marks the laver: of mirrors. The verse displays the women: self-denying. The verse displays the cleansing: the washing. The verse displays the word: the mirror. The making displays the laver of cleansing from surrendered vanity. The verse displays the mirrors: surrendered. The verse displays the laver: the washing. The verse displays the word: reflecting and cleansing. The laver made of the women's mirrors displays the crux: the laver of brass, of the lookingglasses of the women (Exodus 30:18-21; Titus 3:5; James 1:23-25) — the instruments of vanity surrendered for the laver of cleansing, the figure of self-denial and the washing word. The verse displays the court (vv.9-20).
9:
"And he made the court: on the south side southward the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen, an hundred cubits:" The very brief verse notes the making of the court with its linen hangings. The grammatical voices note the court. Henry and Gill note that "he made the court: on the south side southward the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen, an hundred cubits" — the court (the enclosure around the tabernacle) was made with hangings of fine twined linen, a hundred cubits on the south side (Exodus 27:9-19); the court (the outer enclosure, separating the holy space from the common); the white linen hangings (the walls of the court, the figure of purity and righteousness); the dimensions (a hundred cubits, the large court for the people's approach).

On "the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the linen. Wesley and Clarke note the fine twined linen (the white hangings, the walls of the court, Exodus 27:9) — the figure (the white linen, the righteousness of the saints, Revelation 19:8; the purity of God's house, the separation of the holy from the common).

On "an hundred cubits," the grammatical-historical note the court: "an hundred cubits" — the large court (a hundred cubits, the spacious enclosure for the people); the figure (the court the place of the people's approach, separated by the white walls of righteousness); the providence (the court made, the enclosure of God's house, the figure of the separation and purity). The verse displays the making of the court with its linen hangings.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the court — "he made the court... the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen, an hundred cubits," the enclosure (Exodus 27:9-19, Henry, Gill); "the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen," the linen (Revelation 19:8, Wesley, Clarke); "an hundred cubits," the court (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the court. The verse marks the court: made. The verse displays the hangings: fine linen. The verse displays the purity: the white walls. The verse displays the separation: of the holy. The court displays the enclosure of God's house. The verse displays the court: enclosed. The verse displays the linen: white. The verse displays the righteousness: figured. The making of the court with its linen hangings displays the enclosure: the court, the hangings of fine twined linen (Exodus 27:9-19; Revelation 19:8) — the enclosure of God's house, the white walls of righteousness separating the holy from the common. The verse displays the pillars and hangings (vv.10-20).
17:
"And the sockets for the pillars were of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver; and the overlaying of their chapiters of silver; and all the pillars of the court were filleted with silver." The very brief verse notes the sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court pillars. The grammatical voices note the materials. Clarke and Ellicott note that "the sockets for the pillars were of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver... and all the pillars of the court were filleted with silver" — the pillars of the court had sockets (bases) of brass, but hooks, fillets (connecting rods or bands), and chapiters (capitals) overlaid with silver; the brass sockets (the bases in the earth, the lower, baser metal); the silver hooks and fillets (the upper ornaments of silver, the redemption metal); the gradation (brass below, silver above, the figure of the ascent from the common to the holy).

On "the sockets for the pillars were of brass," the witnesses note the bases. Ellicott notes the brass sockets (the bases of the pillars, Exodus 27:10-11) — the foundation (the pillars set in brass).

On "the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver... filleted with silver," the grammatical-historical note the silver: "the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver... all the pillars of the court were filleted with silver" — the silver fillets and chapiters (the connecting silver, the atonement-money silver, vv.25-28); the figure (the silver of redemption binding the pillars, the saints joined by the redemption of Christ); the providence (the pillars filleted with silver, the redemption metal binding God's house). The verse displays the sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court pillars.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the materials — "the sockets for the pillars were of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver... filleted with silver," the gradation (Exodus 27:10-11, Clarke, Ellicott); "the sockets for the pillars were of brass," the bases (Ellicott); "the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver... filleted with silver," the silver (vv.25-28, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the court pillars. The verse marks the sockets: of brass. The verse displays the fillets: of silver. The verse displays the gradation: base to holy. The verse displays the redemption: the silver. The materials display the pillars bound with silver. The verse displays the sockets: brass. The verse displays the silver: binding. The verse displays the redemption: figured. The sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court pillars display the silver of redemption: brass sockets, silver fillets (Exodus 27:10-11; vv.25-28) — the pillars set in brass and bound with the silver of the atonement money, the redemption metal binding God's house. The verse displays the gate of the court (vv.18-20).
21:
"This is the sum of the tabernacle, even of the tabernacle of testimony, as it was counted, according to the commandment of Moses, for the service of the Levites, by the hand of Ithamar, son to Aaron the priest." The witnesses note the faithful reckoning of the tabernacle — the crux of stewardship and accounting. On "This is the sum of the tabernacle... as it was counted, according to the commandment of Moses," interpreters expound the reckoning. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the account — "This is the sum of the tabernacle... as it was counted, according to the commandment of Moses, for the service of the Levites, by the hand of Ithamar": a full account (sum, reckoning) was made of all the materials of the tabernacle, the gold, silver, and brass, counted by the Levites under Ithamar (Aaron's son); the witnesses note the significance — the careful accounting of all that was given for God's house (the materials reckoned up, audited, that all might see the offerings rightly used); the lesson — the faithful stewardship of what is given to God (the leaders giving an account, the offerings of the people not embezzled but openly reckoned, providing for honest things in the sight of all, 2 Corinthians 8:20-21, "avoiding this, that no man should blame us in this abundance which is administered by us; providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men"); the transparency (the open account, that the people might know their gifts were faithfully employed); the figure of faithful stewardship in the house of God.

On "for the service of the Levites, by the hand of Ithamar," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the Levites. Wesley and Henry note the account made by the Levites under Ithamar (the Levites employed in the service, the reckoning by their hand, vv.22-23) — the order (the work and the accounting committed to appointed men); the stewardship (the faithful handling of God's things).

On the sum, the grammatical-historical note the account: the sum of the tabernacle — the full reckoning; the providence (the careful account of God's house, the figure of faithful stewardship and transparency). The verse displays the faithful reckoning of the tabernacle.

The witnesses agree the substance: a full account of the tabernacle's materials was made, by the Levites under Ithamar (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of stewardship: "the sum of the tabernacle... as it was counted" — the careful, open accounting of all that was given for God's house, the faithful stewardship of the offerings (providing for honest things in the sight of all, 2 Corinthians 8:20-21), the transparency that the people might know their gifts were rightly employed, a figure of faithful stewardship in the house of God. The verse displays the stewardship crux. The verse marks the sum: counted. The verse displays the account: faithful. The verse displays the stewardship: open. The verse displays the offerings: rightly used. The reckoning displays the faithful accounting of God's house. The verse displays the sum: reckoned. The verse displays the stewards: the Levites. The verse displays the transparency: of the account. The faithful reckoning of the tabernacle displays the crux: the sum of the tabernacle, as it was counted (2 Corinthians 8:20-21) — the careful, open accounting of all given for God's house, the faithful stewardship of the offerings. The verse displays the workmen (vv.22-23).
22:
"And Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the LORD commanded Moses." The very brief verse notes Bezaleel making all the LORD commanded. The grammatical voices note the workman. Calvin and Gill note that "Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the LORD commanded Moses" — Bezaleel (the chief workman, of Judah, Exodus 35:30) made all that the LORD had commanded Moses; the obedience (the work done exactly as commanded, nothing added or omitted); the faithfulness (Bezaleel making all according to the pattern, Exodus 25:40); the chief workman (the master craftsman completing the work).

On "Bezaleel... of the tribe of Judah," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the workman. Calvin and Keil note Bezaleel of Judah (the chief workman, the royal tribe) — the gift (the Spirit-filled workman, Exodus 31:2-5); the faithfulness (making all as commanded).

On "made all that the LORD commanded Moses," the grammatical-historical note the obedience: "made all that the LORD commanded Moses" — the work done exactly as commanded (all according to the pattern, nothing of his own invention); the figure (the faithful servant doing all God's will, Hebrews 3:5); the providence (the work of God's house done in exact obedience, the pattern followed). The verse displays Bezaleel making all the LORD commanded.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Bezaleel... made all that the LORD commanded Moses," the faithfulness (Exodus 25:40, Calvin, Gill); "Bezaleel... of the tribe of Judah," the workman (Exodus 31:2-5, Calvin, Keil); "made all that the LORD commanded Moses," the obedience (Hebrews 3:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the faithful Bezaleel. The verse marks the workman: Bezaleel. The verse displays the work: all commanded. The verse displays the obedience: exact. The verse displays the pattern: followed. The workman displays Bezaleel doing all as commanded. The verse displays the Bezaleel: faithful. The verse displays the work: complete. The verse displays the obedience: full. Bezaleel making all the LORD commanded displays the faithful work: he made all that the LORD commanded Moses (Exodus 25:40; Hebrews 3:5) — the chief workman doing all in exact obedience, according to the pattern, nothing of his own. The verse displays Aholiab his helper (v.23).
23:
"And with him was Aholiab, son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer in blue, and in purple, and in scarlet, and fine linen." The very brief verse notes Aholiab the skilled helper. The grammatical voices note the workman. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "with him was Aholiab, son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer in blue, and in purple, and in scarlet, and fine linen" — with Bezaleel was Aholiab (of Dan), skilled as an engraver, a cunning (artistic) workman, and an embroiderer in the rich colors and fine linen; the helper (Aholiab, the second master workman, of Dan, Exodus 31:6); the skills (engraving, design, embroidery); the two tribes (Judah and Dan, the chief and his helper, from two tribes).

On "Aholiab, son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan," the witnesses note the helper. Gill notes Aholiab of Dan (the second workman, the helper of Bezaleel) — the partnership (the two workmen, from two tribes, joined in the work).

On "an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer," the grammatical-historical note the skills: "an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer in blue, and in purple, and in scarlet, and fine linen" — the various skills (engraving, design, embroidery, in the rich colors); the figure (the variety of gifts joined in the one work, 1 Corinthians 12:4-11); the providence (Aholiab the skilled helper, the gifts joined for God's house). The verse displays Aholiab the skilled helper.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the helper — "with him was Aholiab... an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer," the skills (Exodus 31:6, Keil, Gill); "Aholiab, son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan," the helper (Gill); "an engraver, and a cunning workman, and an embroiderer," the skills (1 Corinthians 12:4-11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the skilled Aholiab. The verse marks the helper: Aholiab. The verse displays the tribe: Dan. The verse displays the skills: engraving, design, embroidery. The verse displays the gifts: joined. The helper displays the skilled workman joined to Bezaleel. The verse displays the Aholiab: skilled. The verse displays the gifts: various. The verse displays the work: shared. Aholiab the skilled helper displays the joined gifts: Aholiab of Dan, an engraver, cunning workman, and embroiderer (Exodus 31:6; 1 Corinthians 12:4-11) — the skilled helper joined to Bezaleel, the variety of gifts joined in the one work of God's house. The verse displays the gold of the offering (v.24).
24:
"All the gold that was occupied for the work in all the work of the holy place, even the gold of the offering, was twenty and nine talents, and seven hundred and thirty shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary." The witnesses note the reckoning of the gold of the offering. On "all the gold that was occupied for the work... even the gold of the offering," the grammatical voices note the gold. Clarke and Gill note that "all the gold that was occupied for the work in all the work of the holy place, even the gold of the offering, was twenty and nine talents, and seven hundred and thirty shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary" — the total gold used for the work was twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels (a vast sum, by the sanctuary standard); the gold of the offering (the freely given gold, vv.35:22); the great amount (the abundance of the willing offering, the vast treasure given for God's house).

On "the gold of the offering," the reformed and witnesses note the abundance. Gill and Ellicott note the gold of the offering (the freewill gift, the great sum) — the abundance (the people gave so much that there was more than enough, Exodus 36:5-7); the willing giving (the vast treasure freely offered).

On "twenty and nine talents... after the shekel of the sanctuary," the grammatical-historical note the sum: "twenty and nine talents, and seven hundred and thirty shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary" — the exact sum (reckoned by the sanctuary shekel, the holy standard); the figure (the abundance of the offering, the precise accounting, the willing gift faithfully reckoned); the providence (the great sum of gold, the abundance of the willing offering for God's house). The verse displays the reckoning of the gold of the offering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gold — "all the gold that was occupied for the work... even the gold of the offering, was twenty and nine talents," the abundance (Exodus 36:5-7, Clarke, Gill); "the gold of the offering," the abundance (Gill, Ellicott); "twenty and nine talents... after the shekel of the sanctuary," the sum (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reckoned gold. The verse marks the gold: of the offering. The verse displays the sum: vast. The verse displays the abundance: of giving. The verse displays the shekel: of the sanctuary. The gold displays the abundance of the willing offering. The verse displays the gold: reckoned. The verse displays the offering: abundant. The verse displays the giving: willing. The reckoning of the gold of the offering displays the abundance: twenty-nine talents of gold, the gold of the offering (Exodus 36:5-7) — the vast treasure of gold freely given, the abundance of the willing offering for God's house. The verse displays the silver of the atonement money (vv.25-28).
25:
"And the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation was an hundred talents, and a thousand seven hundred and threescore and fifteen shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary:" The witnesses note the silver of the numbered congregation. On "the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation," the grammatical voices note the silver. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation was an hundred talents, and a thousand seven hundred and threescore and fifteen shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary" — the silver came from those numbered (counted in the census), the half-shekel each paid (vv.26); the silver (the atonement money, the half-shekel of every man numbered, Exodus 30:11-16); the sum (a hundred talents and more, by the sanctuary shekel); the source (not a freewill offering like the gold, but the appointed half-shekel of every man numbered).

On "the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation," the reformed and witnesses note the atonement money. Gill notes the silver from those numbered (the half-shekel of the census, the atonement money, Exodus 30:12-16) — the ransom (the half-shekel a ransom for the soul, the redemption money); the figure (every soul ransomed, the redemption price).

On "an hundred talents... after the shekel of the sanctuary," the grammatical-historical note the sum: "an hundred talents, and a thousand seven hundred and threescore and fifteen shekels, after the shekel of the sanctuary" — the exact sum (the silver reckoned, used for the sockets, vv.27); the providence (the atonement money of the numbered congregation, the redemption silver for God's house). The verse displays the silver of the numbered congregation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the silver — "the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation was an hundred talents," the atonement money (Exodus 30:11-16, Keil, Gill); "the silver of them that were numbered of the congregation," the atonement money (Exodus 30:12-16, Gill); "an hundred talents... after the shekel of the sanctuary," the sum (vv.27, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reckoned silver. The verse marks the silver: of the numbered. The verse displays the half-shekel: the atonement money. The verse displays the ransom: for the soul. The verse displays the sum: a hundred talents. The silver displays the atonement money of the numbered. The verse displays the silver: reckoned. The verse displays the ransom: paid. The verse displays the redemption: figured. The silver of the numbered congregation displays the atonement money: the silver of them that were numbered, a hundred talents (Exodus 30:11-16) — the half-shekel of every man numbered, the atonement money, the redemption silver for God's house. The verse displays the half-shekel ransom (v.26).
26:
"A bekah for every man, that is, half a shekel, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for every one that went to be numbered, from twenty years old and upward, for six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty men." The witnesses note the half-shekel ransom for every soul — the crux of the atonement money. On "a bekah for every man, that is, half a shekel... for every one that went to be numbered," interpreters expound the ransom. The reformed and the dominant reading note the atonement money — "a bekah for every man, that is, half a shekel, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for every one that went to be numbered, from twenty years old and upward": every man numbered (from twenty years old and up) paid a bekah, half a shekel, as the atonement money; the witnesses note the significance — the half-shekel a ransom for the soul (the atonement money, "every man a ransom for his soul," Exodus 30:12-16, "that there be no plague among them when thou numberest them"); the equal price (rich and poor alike paid the same half-shekel, the soul of all of equal value, the redemption price the same for all, Exodus 30:15); the figure — the ransom of the soul, not with the half-shekel of silver ultimately, but by the precious blood of Christ (1 Peter 1:18-19, "ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold... but with the precious blood of Christ"; the redemption of the soul costly, Psalm 49:7-8); the six hundred thousand (the vast number numbered, each ransomed, the great congregation redeemed); the silver of the ransom used for the sockets of the sanctuary (vv.27, the redemption the foundation of God's house).

On "from twenty years old and upward, for six hundred thousand and three thousand," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the number. Clarke and Keil note the number numbered (603,550 men, the same as the census, Numbers 1:46) — the vast congregation (each paying the ransom); the figure (the great number redeemed).

On the ransom, the grammatical-historical note the redemption: the half-shekel of every soul — the atonement money, the ransom; the providence (the redemption price of every soul, the figure of the costly ransom by Christ's blood). The verse displays the half-shekel ransom for every soul.

The witnesses agree the substance: every man numbered paid the half-shekel as atonement money, the ransom for his soul (Clarke, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the atonement money: "a bekah for every man... a ransom for his soul" — the half-shekel the redemption price of every soul, rich and poor alike (Exodus 30:12-16), a figure of the ransom of the soul not by silver but by the precious blood of Christ (1 Peter 1:18-19; Psalm 49:7-8), the silver of the ransom the foundation of God's house. The verse displays the atonement-money crux. The verse marks the bekah: the ransom. The verse displays the soul: redeemed. The verse displays the price: equal. The verse displays the Christ: the true ransom. The half-shekel displays the redemption price of every soul. The verse displays the ransom: paid. The verse displays the soul: bought. The verse displays the blood: of Christ. The half-shekel ransom for every soul displays the crux: a bekah for every man, a ransom for his soul (Exodus 30:12-16; 1 Peter 1:18-19; Psalm 49:7-8) — the half-shekel the redemption price of every soul, a figure of the costly ransom by the blood of Christ. The verse displays the sockets cast from the silver (vv.27-28).
27:
"And of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary, and the sockets of the vail; an hundred sockets of the hundred talents, a talent for a socket." The very brief verse notes the sockets cast from the silver of the ransom. The grammatical voices note the sockets. Ellicott and Gill note that "of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary, and the sockets of the vail; an hundred sockets of the hundred talents, a talent for a socket" — the hundred talents of silver (the atonement money) were cast into the hundred sockets (bases) of the sanctuary and the veil, a talent each; the sockets (the silver bases of the boards and the veil-pillars, the foundation of the tabernacle); the silver (the atonement money, the ransom, made the foundation); the figure (the redemption the foundation of God's house, the sanctuary resting on the silver of the ransom).

On "of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary," the reformed and witnesses note the foundation. Gill notes the sockets cast from the atonement silver (the foundation of the tabernacle resting on the ransom money) — the figure (the redemption the foundation, the church built on the ransom of Christ, the sanctuary resting on the atonement); the lesson (the whole structure resting on the silver of the ransom).

On "a talent for a socket," the grammatical-historical note the sockets: "an hundred sockets of the hundred talents, a talent for a socket" — the exact reckoning (a talent each, the hundred sockets); the providence (the atonement silver made the foundation, the figure of redemption underlying God's house). The verse displays the sockets cast from the silver of the ransom.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sockets — "of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary... a talent for a socket," the foundation (Ellicott, Gill); "of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary," the foundation (Gill); "a talent for a socket," the sockets (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the silver sockets. The verse marks the silver: the ransom. The verse displays the sockets: the foundation. The verse displays the sanctuary: resting on it. The verse displays the redemption: the base. The sockets display the foundation of the atonement silver. The verse displays the silver: cast. The verse displays the foundation: the ransom. The verse displays the redemption: underlying. The sockets cast from the silver of the ransom display the foundation: the sockets of the sanctuary cast from the atonement silver (vv.25-26) — the redemption money made the foundation, the sanctuary resting on the silver of the ransom, the figure of redemption underlying God's house. The verse displays the hooks and chapiters (v.28).
28:
"And of the thousand seven hundred seventy and five shekels he made hooks for the pillars, and overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them." The very brief verse notes the silver used for the hooks and chapiters. The grammatical voices note the silver. Ellicott and Gill note that "of the thousand seven hundred seventy and five shekels he made hooks for the pillars, and overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them" — the remaining silver (1,775 shekels, over the hundred talents) was used for the hooks of the pillars, the overlaying of the chapiters (capitals), and the fillets (bands); the silver hooks and fillets (the upper ornaments of the court pillars, vv.17); the redemption silver (the atonement money also adorning the pillars); the figure (the redemption silver binding and adorning God's house, above and around).

On "he made hooks for the pillars," the witnesses note the hooks. Gill notes the silver hooks (for the pillars, from the atonement money) — the use (the remaining silver employed for the ornaments).

On "overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them," the grammatical-historical note the ornaments: "overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them" — the chapiters overlaid and the pillars filleted with silver (the atonement silver adorning, vv.17); the figure (the redemption silver binding and adorning the pillars of God's house); the providence (the atonement money used for the foundation and the ornaments, the redemption underlying and adorning God's house). The verse displays the silver used for the hooks and chapiters.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the silver — "of the thousand seven hundred seventy and five shekels he made hooks for the pillars, and overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them," the ornaments (vv.17, Ellicott, Gill); "he made hooks for the pillars," the hooks (Gill); "overlaid their chapiters, and filleted them," the ornaments (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the silver ornaments. The verse marks the silver: the remainder. The verse displays the hooks: and fillets. The verse displays the chapiters: overlaid. The verse displays the redemption: adorning. The silver displays the atonement money adorning the pillars. The verse displays the silver: used. The verse displays the ornaments: made. The verse displays the redemption: adorning. The silver used for the hooks and chapiters displays the redemption adorning God's house: the silver for the hooks and fillets (vv.17, 27) — the atonement money used not only for the foundation but for the ornaments, the redemption silver binding and adorning the pillars of God's house. The verse displays the brass of the offering (vv.29-31).
29:
"And the brass of the offering was seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels." The very brief verse notes the reckoning of the brass of the offering. The grammatical voices note the brass. Ellicott and Gill note that "the brass of the offering was seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels" — the total brass freely offered was seventy talents and 2,400 shekels; the brass of the offering (the freewill gift of brass, vv.35:24); the sum (the great amount of brass, by the sanctuary standard); the use (the brass for the altar, the laver, the sockets of the court, the pins, vv.30-31).

On "the brass of the offering," the witnesses note the offering. Gill notes the brass of the offering (the freewill gift of brass) — the abundance (the brass freely given, the third of the metals reckoned).

On "seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels," the grammatical-historical note the sum: "seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels" — the exact sum (the brass reckoned, the precise account); the providence (the brass of the offering, the freewill gift, used for the court and the altar, vv.30-31; the careful reckoning of all the metals). The verse displays the reckoning of the brass of the offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the brass — "the brass of the offering was seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels," the offering (vv.35:24, Ellicott, Gill); "the brass of the offering," the offering (Gill); "seventy talents, and two thousand and four hundred shekels," the sum (vv.30-31, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reckoned brass. The verse marks the brass: of the offering. The verse displays the sum: seventy talents. The verse displays the offering: freewill. The verse displays the use: the court and altar. The brass displays the freewill gift of brass reckoned. The verse displays the brass: reckoned. The verse displays the offering: given. The verse displays the account: exact. The reckoning of the brass of the offering displays the freewill gift: seventy talents of brass (vv.35:24; 30-31) — the brass freely given, carefully reckoned, used for the court and the altar of God's house. The verse displays the brass sockets and altar (vv.30-31).
30:
"And therewith he made the sockets to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the brasen altar, and the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar," The very brief verse notes the brass made into the sockets, altar, and vessels. The grammatical voices note the use. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "therewith he made the sockets to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the brasen altar, and the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar" — the brass was made into the sockets of the door of the tabernacle, the brazen altar, its grate, and all its vessels; the use (the brass for the door-sockets, the altar, and its furniture); the brazen altar (made of the offered brass, the place of sacrifice); the vessels (all the furniture of the altar, of brass).

On "the sockets to the door of the tabernacle... and the brasen altar," the witnesses note the use. Gill notes the brass for the door-sockets and the altar (the place of sacrifice, vv.1) — the figure (the brazen altar of sacrifice, the cross).

On "the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar," the grammatical-historical note the furniture: "the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar" — the grate and vessels (the furniture of the altar, vv.3-5); the providence (the brass made into the altar and its furniture, the freewill gift employed for the place of sacrifice). The verse displays the brass made into the sockets, altar, and vessels.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the use — "therewith he made the sockets to the door... and the brasen altar, and the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar," the furniture (Keil, Gill); "the sockets to the door of the tabernacle... and the brasen altar," the altar (vv.1, Gill); "the brasen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar," the furniture (vv.3-5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the brass furniture. The verse marks the brass: the use. The verse displays the altar: brazen. The verse displays the grate: and vessels. The verse displays the sacrifice: the altar. The brass displays the altar and furniture of sacrifice. The verse displays the brass: employed. The verse displays the altar: made. The verse displays the offering: used. The brass made into the sockets, altar, and vessels displays the use: the brazen altar and its vessels (vv.1, 3-5) — the freewill brass employed for the door-sockets, the altar of sacrifice, and all its furniture. The verse displays the court sockets and pins (v.31).
31:
"And the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate, and all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about." The witnesses note the brass made into the court sockets and pins. On "the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate," the grammatical voices note the sockets. Ellicott and Gill note that "the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate, and all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about" — the brass was also made into the sockets (bases) of the court and its gate, and all the pins (tent-pegs) of the tabernacle and the court; the sockets (the brass bases of the court pillars and the gate); the pins (the tent-pegs holding the curtains and hangings firm); the use (the brass for the foundation and the fastenings of the court).

On "the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate," the reformed and witnesses note the foundation. Gill notes the brass sockets of the court and gate (the bases of the pillars, vv.17) — the foundation (the court resting on the brass sockets).

On "all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about," the grammatical-historical note the pins: "all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about" — the pins (the tent-pegs, the small but necessary fastenings, vv.35:18); the figure (the small things holding God's house firm, every part needful, 1 Corinthians 12:22); the providence (the brass made into the sockets and pins, the foundation and fastenings of God's house; the chapter closing the reckoning of the metals, the whole work supplied by the willing offering). The verse displays the brass made into the court sockets and pins.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the use — "the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate, and all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about," the foundation and fastenings (Ellicott, Gill); "the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate," the foundation (vv.17, Gill); "all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about," the pins (1 Corinthians 12:22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the brass sockets and pins. The verse marks the sockets: the foundation. The verse displays the pins: the fastenings. The verse displays the court: held firm. The verse displays the parts: needful. The brass displays the foundation and fastenings of the court. The verse displays the sockets: cast. The verse displays the pins: made. The verse displays the work: supplied. The brass made into the court sockets and pins displays the foundation and fastenings: the sockets and pins of the court (vv.17; 35:18; 1 Corinthians 12:22) — the brass for the bases and the tent-pegs, the foundation and fastenings holding God's house firm, the whole work supplied by the willing offering. The verse closes the chapter, the reckoning of the metals (before the priestly garments, ch.39).
